---
title: "Syllabus"
subtitle: "AEM 7010: Doing Applied Economics Research, Spring 2026"
sidebar: false
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The official course syllabus PDF will be linked here.
:::

## Course summary

AEM 7010 introduces practical skills for doing applied economics research. The course covers four areas across eight 75-minute sessions: trends in economic research, reproducibility, version control with Git and GitHub, and AI tools for coding.

This site documents the last quarter of the class.

> This course provides exposure to a wide variety of basic skills necessary for conducting high-quality research in applied economics. This includes defining a research question, primary and secondary data collection, principles of academic writing and presentation, research ethics, basic coding and data management, as well as project management. This course is meant to help students transition from coursework to the independent research they undertake starting at the end of the first year of the Ph.D. program. Some of the topics (e.g., coding) are only covered at an overview level with the expectation that those requiring a deeper knowledge will take further coursework.
>
> *Source: [Cornell Class Roster, AEM 7010, Spring 2026](https://classes.cornell.edu/browse/roster/SP26/class/AEM/7010).*

## What you will be able to do

By the end of these eight sessions you will be able to:

- Build and document reproducible research workflows.
- Track changes to your R scripts and research code using Git.
- Back up your work on GitHub and collaborate with co-authors through pull requests.
- Tag specific versions of your code for paper submissions and replication packages.
- Use AI coding assistants responsibly in research, with verification.
- Document and disclose AI use in a way that meets journal and replication standards.

## Logistics

- **Term**: Spring 2026, last quarter of the class.
- **Meeting times**: Mondays and Wednesdays, 75-minute sessions.
- **First meeting**: Wednesday, April 8, 2026.
- **Last meeting**: Monday, May 4, 2026.
- **Credits**: 3 credits, S/U.
- **Corequisite**: [ECON 6090](https://courses.cornell.edu/search/?P=ECON%206090).
- **Instructors**: Prof. Ariel Ortiz-Bobea (sessions 1, 4 to 8). Lars Vilhuber, AEA Data Editor (sessions 2 and 3).

## Schedule

The course meets twice a week: Mondays and Wednesdays. Sessions 2 and 3 are guest-led by [Lars Vilhuber](https://www.ilr.cornell.edu/people/lars-vilhuber), AEA Data Editor. The other six are taught by Ariel Ortiz-Bobea.

```{=html}
<style>
.session-list {
  list-style: none;
  padding-left: 0;
  margin: 1.5rem 0 0;
  border-top: 1px solid #e2e2dc;
}
.session-list li {
  position: relative;
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 2.5rem 8rem 1fr;
  gap: 1rem;
  padding: 0.85rem 0.75rem;
  border-bottom: 1px solid #e2e2dc;
  align-items: baseline;
  transition: background 0.15s ease;
  border-radius: 4px;
}
.session-list li:hover { background: #F7F4E9; }
.session-list .num {
  font-weight: 700;
  color: #B31B1B;
  font-size: 0.95rem;
}
.session-list .date {
  color: #6B6B6B;
  font-size: 0.92rem;
  font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums;
}
.session-list .topic a {
  font-weight: 600;
  color: #1B1B1B;
  text-decoration: none;
  border-bottom: 1px dotted #B31B1B;
}
.session-list .topic a::before {
  content: "";
  position: absolute;
  inset: 0;
  cursor: pointer;
  z-index: 0;
}
.session-list li:hover .topic a {
  color: #B31B1B;
  border-bottom-color: #B31B1B;
}
.session-list .topic .note {
  display: block;
  font-weight: 400;
  font-size: 0.88rem;
  color: #6B6B6B;
  margin-top: 0.15rem;
}
@media (max-width: 600px) {
  .session-list li { grid-template-columns: 2.5rem 1fr; }
  .session-list .date { grid-column: 2; font-size: 0.85rem; margin-bottom: 0.1rem; }
  .session-list .topic { grid-column: 1 / -1; padding-left: 2.5rem; }
}
</style>

<ul class="session-list">
  <li>
    <span class="num">1.</span>
    <span class="date">Wed, Apr 8</span>
    <span class="topic"><a href="intro/session1.html">Trends in Economic Research</a><span class="note">Reproducibility expectations, the data revolution, and the rise of AI tools.</span></span>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="num">2.</span>
    <span class="date">Mon, Apr 13</span>
    <span class="topic"><a href="reproducibility/session2.html">Reproducibility I</a><span class="note">Foundations and what top journals require. With Lars Vilhuber.</span></span>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="num">3.</span>
    <span class="date">Wed, Apr 15</span>
    <span class="topic"><a href="reproducibility/session3.html">Reproducibility II</a><span class="note">Replication packages in practice. Restricted data and computational environments. With Lars Vilhuber.</span></span>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="num">4.</span>
    <span class="date">Mon, Apr 20</span>
    <span class="topic"><a href="version-control/session4.html">Git Fundamentals</a><span class="note">The core Git workflow on your own computer.</span></span>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="num">5.</span>
    <span class="date">Wed, Apr 22</span>
    <span class="topic"><a href="version-control/session5.html">GitHub and Collaboration</a><span class="note">Push, pull, branches, pull requests, tags for replication packages.</span></span>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="num">6.</span>
    <span class="date">Mon, Apr 27</span>
    <span class="topic"><a href="ai-tools/session6.html">AI Tools I: Chat</a><span class="note">A mental model of LLMs. Live demo of a chat-driven scrape.</span></span>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="num">7.</span>
    <span class="date">Wed, Apr 29</span>
    <span class="topic"><a href="ai-tools/session7.html">AI Tools II: Cowork</a><span class="note">Agentic desktop AI that can see your files and run code.</span></span>
  </li>
  <li>
    <span class="num">8.</span>
    <span class="date">Mon, May 4</span>
    <span class="topic"><a href="ai-tools/session8.html">AI Tools III: Claude Code</a><span class="note">A code-native agent inside a git project.</span></span>
  </li>
</ul>
```

### Before each session

Each session has a short *Before class* note at the top of its page. Read it the day before. The first one to check is for [Session 4](version-control/session4.qmd), which assumes you have completed the [Setup](setup.qmd) page.

## Grading and expectations

Attendance is expected at every session. Each session lists short tasks under *Before class*, *In class*, and *After class*. Completing these is what builds the practical fluency the course is designed to deliver.

## Before you begin

Two short setup steps before Session 4: install Git, R, and an editor. Details on the [Setup](setup.qmd) page.
